body_txt: Something should be subtitled for the audience to be reasonably expected to understand it, but it isn't. This may be used to make a point of the Language Barrier — our viewpoint character can't understand what's being said, so neither can we, forcing us to empathise with their confusion or isolation. If this is the case, languages the character does understand will be subtitled. People who use subtitles for accessibility are often very irritated by this — after all, if someone speaking Spanish is subtitled [SPEAKS SPANISH] (or worse, [SPEAKS FOREIGN LANGUAGE]), and you understand Spanish but simply can't hear the TV, the subtitler has just basically decreed that you are not allowed to understand something you would otherwise have understood, which is the opposite of what they're supposed to do. People who see it this way may argue that the caption should simply have the dialogue in Spanish — that way, whoever in the audience wouldn't have understood the dialogue still won't, and whoever would have, will. Compare Fun with Subtitles . This is related to Bilingual Bonus and Viewers Are Geniuses . Compare Even the Subtitler Is Stumped , when subtitles are attempted but impossible due to comic unintelligibility.
